title: Cyber Security (Risk Assessment and Audit) Regulations 2024
auditNote: Section references corrected 2026-03-04 (original file had systematic offset error: all risk-assessment provisions referenced s27 instead of s21, all audit provisions referenced s28 instead of s23). Specific quantitative values (insurance thresholds, experience years, processing timelines, frequency periods) are indicative and have NOT been verified against the official P.U.(A) 292/2024 gazette text. Do not rely on any specific figure without independent verification.
subject: Establishes requirements for periodic cyber security risk assessments and audits of national critical information infrastructure, including frequency, approved assessor and auditor qualifications, methodology, report contents, and submission timelines

provisions.title: Citation and Commencement
provisions.requirement: These regulations may be cited as the Cyber Security (Risk Assessment and Audit) Regulations 2024 and shall come into operation on 26 August 2024.
provisions.explanation: Establishes the formal name and commencement date of these regulations, aligning with the parent Act's effective date.

provisions.title: Interpretation
provisions.requirement: In these Regulations, unless the context otherwise requires: 'approved assessor' means a person registered under regulation 5 to conduct cyber security risk assessments; 'approved auditor' means a person registered under regulation 10 to conduct cyber security audits; 'assessment cycle' means the period between two consecutive risk assessments; 'audit cycle' means the period between two consecutive audits; 'Register of Approved Assessors' means the register maintained by the Chief Executive under regulation 6; 'Register of Approved Auditors' means the register maintained by the Chief Executive under regulation 11; 'risk assessment report' means the report prepared under regulation 8; 'audit report' means the report prepared under regulation 13.
provisions.explanation: Defines key terms that distinguish between risk assessments and audits -- two distinct but complementary compliance obligations. The regulation establishes separate approval regimes for assessors and auditors, reflecting the different skill sets required for each function.

provisions.title: Risk Assessment Frequency
provisions.requirement: Every national critical information infrastructure entity shall cause a cyber security risk assessment to be conducted on its national critical information infrastructure at intervals not exceeding twelve months from the date of the completion of the previous risk assessment. The first risk assessment shall be completed within twelve months of the date on which the entity is designated as a national critical information infrastructure entity under section 18 of the Act. Where a material change occurs to the entity's national critical information infrastructure, including but not limited to significant system upgrades, network architecture changes, migration to cloud infrastructure, or integration of new operational technology systems, the entity shall cause an additional risk assessment to be conducted within ninety days of the material change, regardless of when the previous assessment was completed.
provisions.explanation: The 12-month maximum cycle ensures that risk assessments remain current and reflect the evolving threat landscape. Newly designated NCII entities have 12 months from designation to complete their first assessment, providing reasonable time to engage an approved assessor. Critically, material changes to infrastructure trigger an additional out-of-cycle assessment within 90 days. This prevents entities from making significant changes and operating with an outdated risk profile until the next scheduled assessment.
provisions.verificationStatus: specific-values-not-verified: 12-month cycle, 90-day material-change trigger — verify against official gazette

provisions.title: Approved Assessor Requirements
provisions.requirement: A cyber security risk assessment under these Regulations shall be conducted only by an approved assessor. No person shall act as an approved assessor unless the person: (a) is registered in the Register of Approved Assessors maintained by the Chief Executive; (b) holds a valid and current registration that has not been suspended or revoked; (c) is independent of the entity being assessed, meaning the assessor has no financial interest in, employment relationship with, or material business relationship with the entity other than the engagement for the risk assessment; (d) maintains professional indemnity insurance coverage of not less than [amount to be verified against gazette]. The Chief Executive may impose additional conditions on the registration of an approved assessor.
provisions.explanation: Independence is the cornerstone requirement -- assessors cannot have financial ties to the entity being assessed. This prevents conflicts of interest that could compromise assessment integrity. A minimum professional indemnity insurance requirement applies to protect NCII entities against negligent assessments (specific amount not verified against official gazette — original file stated RM2,000,000 but this figure cannot be confirmed). Only persons on NACSA's official register may conduct assessments, giving NACSA direct oversight of assessor quality.
provisions.verificationStatus: specific-values-not-verified: PI insurance threshold — original indicative value RM2,000,000 replaced with placeholder pending gazette verification

provisions.title: Registration as Approved Assessor
provisions.requirement: A person may apply to the Chief Executive for registration as an approved assessor by submitting an application in the prescribed form together with: (a) evidence of relevant professional qualifications, which shall include at minimum one of the following: Certified Information Systems Security Professional (CISSP), Certified Information Security Manager (CISM), Certified Risk and Information Systems Control (CRISC), or an equivalent qualification recognised by the Chief Executive; (b) evidence of not less than [years to be verified] of professional experience in cyber security risk assessment, information security management, or a related field; (c) evidence of professional indemnity insurance as required under regulation 4(d); (d) a statutory declaration that the applicant has not been convicted of any offence involving fraud, dishonesty, or moral turpitude; (e) the prescribed registration fee. The Chief Executive shall, within [processing period to be verified] of receiving a complete application, either approve the application and enter the applicant's name in the Register or refuse the application with written reasons.
provisions.explanation: Sets a high professional bar for assessors: recognised certifications plus a minimum number of years of relevant experience (specific figure not verified against gazette — original file stated 5 years and 60-day processing period). The character requirement (no fraud or dishonesty convictions) ensures assessors are trustworthy given their access to sensitive NCII information.
provisions.verificationStatus: specific-values-not-verified: experience years (originally stated 5), processing timeline (originally stated 60 days), certification list — verify against official gazette

provisions.title: Register of Approved Assessors
provisions.requirement: The Chief Executive shall establish and maintain a Register of Approved Assessors, which shall contain: (a) the name and business address of each approved assessor; (b) the qualifications and relevant experience of each approved assessor; (c) the date of registration and the date of expiry of registration; (d) any conditions imposed on the registration; (e) any suspension or revocation of registration and the reasons therefor. The Register or a summary thereof shall be published on the NACSA portal and updated at least once every calendar quarter. Registration as an approved assessor shall be valid for a period of [duration to be verified] from the date of registration and may be renewed upon application.
provisions.explanation: The public register serves a dual purpose: it allows NCII entities to identify and verify approved assessors, and it provides transparency and accountability. The registration period (original file stated 3 years) is subject to gazette verification. Quarterly updates to the published register ensure NCII entities have access to current information.
provisions.verificationStatus: specific-values-not-verified: registration duration (originally stated 3 years) — verify against official gazette

provisions.title: Risk Assessment Methodology Requirements
provisions.requirement: Every risk assessment conducted under these Regulations shall: (a) adopt a methodology that is consistent with internationally recognised risk management frameworks, including but not limited to ISO/IEC 27005, NIST SP 800-30, or any framework specified by the Chief Executive; (b) identify and document all assets forming part of the national critical information infrastructure, including hardware, software, data, networks, personnel, and physical facilities; (c) identify and evaluate threats relevant to the national critical information infrastructure, taking into account the current threat landscape as published by NACSA and sector-specific threat intelligence; (d) identify and evaluate vulnerabilities in the national critical information infrastructure through both automated scanning and manual assessment techniques; (e) assess the likelihood and potential impact of identified risks using a consistent and documented rating methodology; (f) evaluate the adequacy and effectiveness of existing security controls; (g) determine residual risk levels after accounting for existing controls; (h) provide prioritised recommendations for risk treatment, including specific remediation actions, timelines, and resource requirements; (i) consider interdependencies between the entity's national critical information infrastructure and other critical infrastructure sectors.
provisions.explanation: The methodology requirements ensure a comprehensive and systematic approach to risk assessment. By referencing international standards (ISO 27005, NIST 800-30) while allowing the Chief Executive to specify alternatives, the regulation is both rigorous and flexible. The requirement to consider NACSA-published threat intelligence integrates national threat awareness into entity-level assessments. The interdependency analysis in paragraph (i) is particularly important for understanding cascading risks across NCII sectors.

provisions.title: Risk Assessment Report Contents
provisions.requirement: The risk assessment report shall contain: (a) an executive summary suitable for review by senior management and the board of directors; (b) a description of the scope and boundaries of the assessment; (c) the methodology adopted, including the risk rating criteria used; (d) a complete asset inventory of the national critical information infrastructure within scope; (e) a threat assessment based on current threat intelligence; (f) a vulnerability assessment including the results of technical scanning and manual testing; (g) a risk register listing all identified risks with their likelihood, impact, and risk rating; (h) an evaluation of the effectiveness of existing security controls mapped against each identified risk; (i) a residual risk assessment; (j) prioritised recommendations for risk treatment with proposed timelines and estimated resource requirements; (k) a comparison with the findings of the previous risk assessment, if any, including progress on previously recommended remediation actions; (l) a declaration by the approved assessor confirming the independence and completeness of the assessment; (m) any limitations or constraints that affected the conduct or scope of the assessment.
provisions.explanation: The report must serve both technical and governance purposes: the executive summary enables board-level oversight, while the detailed technical sections provide actionable information for security teams. The requirement to compare against previous assessments (paragraph k) creates accountability for remediation progress. The assessor's independence declaration (paragraph l) and the disclosure of limitations (paragraph m) promote transparency and integrity in the assessment process.

provisions.title: Audit Frequency
provisions.requirement: Every national critical information infrastructure entity shall cause a cyber security audit to be conducted on its national critical information infrastructure at intervals not exceeding twelve months from the date of the completion of the previous audit. The first audit shall be completed within [period to be verified] of the date on which the entity is designated as a national critical information infrastructure entity under section 18 of the Act. The Chief Executive may, by written notice to an entity, require the entity to cause an additional audit to be conducted within a period specified in the notice where: (a) the entity has experienced a cyber security incident with significant impact; (b) a risk assessment has identified critical or high-risk findings that require independent verification; (c) the Chief Executive has reasonable grounds to believe that the entity is not complying with the Act, these Regulations, or any direction issued under the Act.
provisions.explanation: Audits follow a 12-month cycle similar to risk assessments. The grace period for a newly designated entity's first audit is subject to gazette verification (original file stated 18 months, which is longer than the 12-month risk assessment window, on the basis that entities should complete an assessment before being audited). The Chief Executive retains power to mandate additional audits in the three specified circumstances.
provisions.verificationStatus: specific-values-not-verified: first-audit grace period (originally stated 18 months), 12-month audit cycle — verify against official gazette

provisions.title: Approved Auditor Requirements
provisions.requirement: A cyber security audit under these Regulations shall be conducted only by an approved auditor. No person shall act as an approved auditor unless the person: (a) is registered in the Register of Approved Auditors maintained by the Chief Executive; (b) holds a valid and current registration that has not been suspended or revoked; (c) is independent of the entity being audited, meaning the auditor has no financial interest in, employment relationship with, or material business relationship with the entity other than the engagement for the audit; (d) has not acted as the approved assessor for the same entity's most recent risk assessment; (e) maintains professional indemnity insurance coverage of not less than [amount to be verified against gazette]. A person may be registered as both an approved assessor and an approved auditor, provided that the person shall not perform both functions for the same entity within the same twelve-month period.
provisions.explanation: In addition to the independence requirements that mirror those for assessors, auditors face an additional restriction: they cannot have conducted the most recent risk assessment for the same entity. This separation prevents the scenario where an auditor is effectively reviewing their own prior work. However, the regulation pragmatically allows individuals to hold both registrations -- they simply cannot perform both roles for the same entity within 12 months. The PI insurance threshold (original file stated RM2,000,000) requires gazette verification.
provisions.verificationStatus: specific-values-not-verified: PI insurance threshold (originally stated RM2,000,000) — verify against official gazette

provisions.title: Register of Approved Auditors
provisions.requirement: The Chief Executive shall establish and maintain a Register of Approved Auditors, which shall contain: (a) the name and business address of each approved auditor; (b) the qualifications and relevant experience of each approved auditor; (c) the date of registration and the date of expiry of registration; (d) any conditions imposed on the registration; (e) any suspension or revocation of registration and the reasons therefor. The Register or a summary thereof shall be published on the NACSA portal and updated at least once every calendar quarter. Registration as an approved auditor shall be valid for a period of [duration to be verified] from the date of registration and may be renewed upon application.
provisions.explanation: Mirrors the Register of Approved Assessors in structure and transparency requirements. Published quarterly on the NACSA portal so NCII entities can readily identify approved auditors. The registration duration (original file stated 3 years) requires gazette verification.
provisions.verificationStatus: specific-values-not-verified: registration duration (originally stated 3 years) — verify against official gazette

provisions.title: Audit Scope Requirements
provisions.requirement: Every audit conducted under these Regulations shall evaluate: (a) the entity's compliance with the provisions of the Act, these Regulations, and any directions or codes of practice issued by the Chief Executive; (b) the entity's compliance with the standards and measures for cyber security specified in the entity's direction under section 24 of the Act; (c) the adequacy, effectiveness, and operational implementation of the entity's cyber security policies, procedures, and processes; (d) the entity's implementation of recommendations from the most recent risk assessment report; (e) the entity's incident response capability, including testing of the incident response plan through tabletop or simulated exercises where practicable; (f) the entity's access control, identity management, and authentication mechanisms; (g) the entity's network security architecture, including segmentation, monitoring, and intrusion detection capabilities; (h) the entity's data protection and backup practices, including the integrity and recoverability of backup systems; (i) the entity's vulnerability management programme, including patching timeliness and coverage; (j) the entity's security awareness and training programme for personnel; (k) the entity's third-party and supply chain cyber security risk management practices; (l) the entity's business continuity and disaster recovery arrangements as they relate to the national critical information infrastructure.
provisions.explanation: The audit scope is deliberately comprehensive, covering legal compliance, technical controls, operational processes, and organisational governance. Notably, it includes evaluation of risk assessment remediation progress (paragraph d), ensuring that risk assessments produce real improvements rather than shelf-ware reports. The inclusion of supply chain risk management (paragraph k) reflects the modern reality that NCII security depends on the entire ecosystem, not just the entity itself.

provisions.title: Audit Report Contents
provisions.requirement: The audit report shall contain: (a) an executive summary suitable for review by senior management and the board of directors; (b) a description of the scope and methodology of the audit; (c) a statement of compliance or non-compliance with each applicable provision of the Act, these Regulations, and any directions or codes of practice issued by the Chief Executive; (d) detailed findings for each area evaluated under regulation 12, including evidence examined, observations, and conclusions; (e) a classification of each finding as critical, major, minor, or observation, using criteria published by the Chief Executive; (f) an assessment of the entity's progress in implementing recommendations from the most recent risk assessment and any previous audit; (g) specific and actionable recommendations for addressing each finding, with proposed timelines; (h) an overall assessment of the entity's cyber security posture, expressed as a maturity rating using the maturity model published by the Chief Executive; (i) a declaration by the approved auditor confirming the independence and completeness of the audit; (j) any limitations or constraints that affected the conduct or scope of the audit.
provisions.explanation: The audit report structure ensures consistency and comparability across NCII entities. The maturity rating requirement (paragraph h) enables NACSA to benchmark entity security postures across sectors and track national-level trends over time. The finding classification system (paragraph e) helps entities prioritise remediation and enables NACSA to focus supervisory attention on entities with critical findings.

provisions.title: Submission Timelines
provisions.requirement: The entity shall submit the risk assessment report to the Chief Executive within thirty days of the completion of the risk assessment. The entity shall submit the audit report to the Chief Executive within thirty days of the completion of the audit. Where the Chief Executive requests additional information or clarification in relation to a risk assessment report or audit report, the entity shall provide the requested information within fourteen days of the request. The entity shall, within sixty days of receiving the risk assessment report or audit report, submit to the Chief Executive a remediation plan addressing all critical and major findings identified in the report, including specific remediation actions, responsible persons, target completion dates, and resource allocation.
provisions.explanation: The 30-day submission window gives entities time to review reports before forwarding them to NACSA, while ensuring timely regulatory oversight. The mandatory 60-day remediation plan for critical and major findings creates accountability for addressing identified weaknesses. Specific timelines (30 days, 14 days, 60 days) require gazette verification.
provisions.verificationStatus: specific-values-not-verified: submission timelines (30 days, 14 days, 60 days) — verify against official gazette

provisions.title: Penalty for Non-Compliance
provisions.requirement: Any national critical information infrastructure entity that fails to comply with regulation 3, 9, or 14 commits an offence and shall, on conviction, be liable to a fine not exceeding [amount — see note] or, in the case of a continuing offence, to a further fine not exceeding [daily amount — see note] for every day during which the offence continues after conviction.
provisions.explanation: IMPORTANT — PENALTY CONFLICT: The penalty figures in this regulation (original file stated RM200,000 + RM5,000 daily) conflict with the penalties/index.json entries for the same conduct: s21(4) and s23(4) are listed there as RM500,000 and 10 years' imprisonment. Two possible explanations: (1) this regulation creates a lower-tier regulatory offence separate from the Act's criminal offence; or (2) the figures are AI-generated errors. In either case, the specific amounts must be verified against the official gazette before reliance. The Act-level penalties at s21(4) and s23(4) are the controlling maximum under the parent legislation.
provisions.verificationStatus: specific-values-not-verified: penalty quantum conflicts with penalties/index.json — gazette verification mandatory
